cards.text: A {{c1::container}} is a data type that provides storage for a collection of {{c2::unnamed}} objects (called {{c3::elements}}).
In a container, the individual values stored are called {{c1::elements}}, and the number of elements is called the container's {{c2::length}} or {{c2::count}}.

cards.front: Why are elements in a container unnamed?
cards.back: So that we can put as many elements in the container as we want without having to give each element a unique name. The container provides methods to access elements without needing individual names.

cards.text: In C++, containers are {{c1::homogenous}}, meaning all elements must have the {{c2::same type}}.
Arrays store elements {{c1::contiguously}}, meaning each element is placed in an {{c2::adjacent memory location}}, with {{c3::no gaps}}.
C++ has three primary array types: {{c1::C-style arrays}}, {{c2::std::vector}}, and {{c3::std::array}}.

cards.back: A class type that implements a container (part of C++ Containers library)

cards.front: Why is std::string not considered a true container in C++?
cards.back: It doesn't implement all the requirements listed in the C++ standard for containers. However, it behaves like a container in most circumstances, so it's called a "pseudo-container".

cards.text: {{c1::std::vector}} was introduced in C++03 to make arrays safer and easier to use, while {{c2::std::array}} was introduced in C++11 as a direct replacement for C-style arrays.

cards.concept: When to use containers instead of individual variables
cards.explanation:
Use containers when you need to work with a set of related values. Individual variables don't scale well:
  • Each variable needs a unique name
  • Can't easily iterate over values
  • Hard to maintain and modify (e.g., adding one more value requires changing code everywhere)
  • Cannot determine count programmatically
examples: int scores[30];  // Better than 30 individual variables
std::vector<int> scores;  // Even better: can grow/shrink

cards.front: What is the difference between "length" and "size" when referring to containers?
cards.back: "Length" refers to the number of elements in a container. "Size" refers to the amount of storage (in bytes) required by an object. This distinction avoids confusion with the sizeof operator.

cards.text: Common container operations include: {{c1::create}} a container, {{c2::access}} elements, {{c3::insert and remove}} elements, and {{c4::get the number}} of elements.
